mappings.rationaleAI: The Tang dynasty founding by the Li family, early Tang administration, fiscal and legal systems, T'ai Tsung's reforms, conquest of eastern Turks, and Empress Wu's influence all directly address the early T'ang period (D.2.c). The An Lushan rebellion (755) marks the endpoint of this section.
The Silk Road's Han dynasty origins and its role facilitating trade between China and Central Asia correspond to Han foreign relations and Pan Ch'ao's campaigns in Central Asia (C.2.c.ii).

Qin Shi Huang's unification of the Warring States, founding of the Ch'in dynasty, abolition of feudalism, Legalist governance, Great Wall construction, highway building, and standardization reforms directly address the establishment of the Ch'in empire (C.1), early Ch'in successes (C.1.a), and Ch'in strategy and unification (C.1.b).

The Han dynasty article covers the Western Han founding by Liu Pang, Wang Mang usurpation (C.2.a), Eastern Han restoration (C.2.b), Confucian ideology, Xiongnu campaigns and Central Asian expansion, Silk Road establishment, and technological innovations including papermaking (C.2.c).

Covers East Asia (China) in the ancient period including Bronze and Iron Age developments, providing broad context for Chinese civilization's character (A) and the emergence of traditional Chinese civilization (B).

The post-classical period (c. 500-1450) overlaps with the Six Dynasties period, Sui reunification, and early Tang era, covering the breakdown and revival of the empire (D) including the spread of Buddhism and Tang dynasty China.

A comprehensive country overview spanning Paleolithic habitation through dynastic rule including Qin, Han, and Tang, providing broad coverage of Chinese civilization's character (A), its emergence (B), unification under Ch'in and Han (C), and breakdown and revival (D).

Confucius's teachings on personal morality, filial piety, and governmental virtue during the Spring and Autumn period directly address the classical period of Chinese literature and philosophy, specifically Confucianism as one of the 'hundred schools' (B.2.e).
Laozi as the legendary founder of Taoism and the Tao Te Ching's articulation of the Dao and wu wei directly address Taoism within the classical period of Chinese philosophy (B.2.e).

Taoism's philosophical foundations in the Tao Te Ching and Zhuangzi relate to the classical period of Chinese philosophy (B.2.e), while Taoist resurgence during the Six Dynasties period is covered under intellectual and religious trends after the Han collapse (D.1.b).
Coverage of Confucianism, Taoism, Legalism, and Mohism from China directly relates to the classical period of Chinese literature and philosophy and the 'hundred schools' (B.2.e).

The Paleolithic and Mesolithic stages in North China and Neolithic developments correspond to the prehistoric period of Chinese civilization (B.1), including Paleolithic industries (B.1.a) and Neolithic domestication (B.1.b).
Bronze Age developments in East Asia, including early bronze objects in the Ordos region (B.1.d) and the Shang period bronze culture at Anyang (B.2.b), are directly relevant.

The spread of iron technology in East Asia during the Warring States period contributed to social, political, and economic changes including decline of feudalism, urbanization, and economic development (B.2.d.ii).

Asian history's coverage of Chinese classical dynasties (Zhou, Qin, Han) and the Silk Road provides broad context across the character of Chinese civilization (A), its emergence (B), Ch'in and Han unification (C), and breakdown and revival through the Tang (D).
General survey touching on Chinese civilization among early civilizations and Axial Age philosophical systems, providing broad context for the character and achievements of Chinese civilization (A).
